description: Continuous spoken lecture narration for TTS. Symbols spelled for speech; no UI drag cues; light bridges between slides.

slides.title: Slicing Spacetime
slides.voiceover: Welcome. This lecture is called Slicing Spacetime. In nineteen fifteen, Einstein described gravity with nothing but pen and paper. His equations are among the most beautiful in all of physics — and also among the hardest to solve. That difficulty is the heart of today's story. For almost a century it stopped us from answering a simple question: what does a black hole collision actually look like? By the end you will see how supercomputers and GPUs finally turned Einstein's impossible equations into a working laboratory for the cosmos.

slides.title: AI Winter ↔ NR Dark Ages
slides.voiceover: Start with the big picture, because it repeats a familiar pattern. Artificial intelligence and numerical relativity both lived through decades-long dark ages. In each case the theory already existed on paper, but the algorithms and the hardware simply could not deliver an answer. And in each case the breakthrough came the same way: better mathematics, finally paired with GPUs and massive compute. Keep one path in mind for the rest of the talk — A D M, then B S S N, then C C Z four, the two thousand five breakthrough, and LIGO in two thousand fifteen. We will earn each of those names one at a time.

slides.title: The Missing Waveforms
slides.voiceover: So why do we need numerical relativity at all? Here is the concrete reason. Einstein predicted gravitational waves in nineteen sixteen, yet for nearly a hundred years nobody could compute what a black hole merger actually sounds like. Pen-and-paper approximations handle the gentle inspiral, and perturbation theory handles the final ringdown. But the merger itself — the loudest, most informative moment — is violently non-linear, and no approximation survives it. Only full numerical relativity can fill in that gap. When LIGO caught its first signal, it was matched against roughly two hundred thousand numerical templates. Without those templates, gravitational-wave astronomy would be deaf. That missing piece is what the whole field was built to compute.

slides.title: The Problem with Newton
slides.voiceover: But before we can simulate gravity, we need the right theory of gravity — and Newton's is not it. Newton's gravity acts instantly across any distance. If the Sun vanished right now, Earth would change its orbit in the same instant, even though the Sun's light takes about eight minutes to reach us. That is a problem, because special relativity says nothing — not even a change in gravity — can travel faster than light. Einstein spent a decade repairing this. In general relativity, gravity travels at exactly the speed of light. If the Sun vanished, Earth would keep orbiting for about eight point three minutes, until the news of its disappearance finally arrived.

slides.title: Einstein Built on Differential Geometry
slides.voiceover: And Einstein did not build this theory from nothing. The mathematics of curved space was already waiting for him, assembled over the previous century. Gauss showed how to measure the curvature of a surface from within it. Riemann extended that to spaces of any dimension equipped with a metric — the direct ancestor of Einstein's metric tensor. Christoffel added connection symbols that track how directions twist as you move. Ricci and Levi-Civita packaged it all into tensor calculus, so the laws read the same in every coordinate system. Einstein's own leap was physical, not mathematical: he realized that this geometry is gravity. Spacetime is the stage, and its curvature is the force.

slides.title: Einstein's Field Equation
slides.voiceover: Here is that idea written as a single line: Einstein's field equation. Read it as a sentence. The left side is pure geometry — how spacetime curves. The right side is matter and energy — how much is present and how it moves. In plain words: matter tells spacetime how to curve, and curved spacetime tells matter how to move. It looks as innocent as E equals m c squared. That is the trap. This one tidy line is actually shorthand for ten equations tangled together.

slides.title: 10 Coupled Non-Linear PDEs
slides.voiceover: Unfold that shorthand and the single line shatters into ten coupled, non-linear partial differential equations. Two words there are the villains of this talk. Coupled means you cannot solve any one equation by itself — they are all locked together. Non-linear means gravity feeds back on itself, so the answer depends on the answer. Pen and paper can handle only the most idealized, symmetric cases. Anything that actually moves and changes — a real merger — needs a machine. Hold onto that word non-linear; it is why we are here.

slides.title: The Metric Tensor
slides.voiceover: Let's unpack the geometry gently, one object at a time, starting with the metric. On a flat table, distance is just Pythagoras: d s squared equals d x squared plus d y squared. In spacetime we write d s squared equals g mu nu times d x mu times d x nu. Think of it this way: coordinates are only addresses, like labels on a map. The metric is the map's scale bar — it converts a difference in addresses into a real distance or a real span of time. And here is the key: when that scale bar changes from place to place, that change is gravity. Gravity is geometry, not a mysterious pulling force.

slides.title: How $ds$ Changes Near a Mass
slides.voiceover: Let's see that scale bar in action. In flat space, every little ruler has the same length everywhere, so the real distance always matches the coordinate distance. Near a mass, something changes: the very same gap between coordinates can stretch into a much larger real distance. Far from the mass the two still agree, and everything looks ordinary. It is only close to the mass that curved space visibly stretches. That growing gap between address and reality — that is the metric doing gravity's work.

slides.title: Christoffel Symbols
slides.voiceover: Once distances vary from place to place, even the meaning of going straight starts to drift — and that is what Christoffel symbols keep track of. Picture them as road-map corrections. On a flat table, forward points the same way everywhere. On a globe, forward at the equator and forward at the pole are completely different directions. Christoffel symbols measure exactly how forward twists as you move. In flat space they vanish. Near a black hole they become enormous. They are the raw ingredients of curvature — but not the curvature itself, not just yet.

slides.title: Parallel Transport
slides.voiceover: There is a beautiful way to make curvature visible, and it uses those twisting directions directly. Take an arrow and carry it around a closed loop, keeping it as straight as you possibly can at every step — this is called parallel transport. On a flat surface, the arrow comes home pointing exactly as it started. On a sphere, it comes home rotated. That rotation appeared out of nowhere, purely from the trip. And crucially, the amount of rotation depends on the path you took. That path-dependence is the very definition of curvature.

slides.title: Riemann Curvature Tensor
slides.voiceover: Bundle all of that path-dependence together and you get the Riemann curvature tensor — the complete answer to how curved spacetime is at a given point. What does it mean physically? It measures tidal forces: whether nearby, freely falling objects drift apart or squeeze together. Drop two balls toward Earth and they slowly move closer, because gravity points toward the center and their paths converge. That relative drift, and nothing more, is curvature. And Riemann is built entirely from the Christoffel symbols and how they change.

slides.title: Geodesic Deviation
slides.voiceover: Geodesic deviation tells the same story as a simple picture. Two walkers start side by side and each just walks straight ahead, never turning. On a flat table they stay perfectly parallel forever. On a sphere, or in curved spacetime, they drift together or apart even though neither one steers. The rate of that drift is precisely what the Riemann tensor measures. And notice what is missing: there is no force between the walkers. The space between them is simply curved.

slides.title: Ricci Tensor & Scalar
slides.voiceover: Riemann carries the full detail, which is often more than we need, so we compress it. First into the Ricci tensor, then into a single number, the Ricci scalar, and finally into the Einstein tensor, written capital G. That G is assembled with great care, so that energy is automatically conserved for free. And this is exactly the left-hand side of Einstein's equation — the geometry side we started with.

slides.title: Stress-Energy Tensor
slides.voiceover: Now the right-hand side: the stress-energy tensor. It answers one question — what is here, and how is it moving? For ordinary matter, that means density, pressure, and velocity. For the exotic matter that props open a wormhole, it comes instead from a scalar field, with energy and pressure set by how that field varies and by its potential. The headline difference is simple: normal matter attracts, while exotic matter, with its negative energy, can effectively push space apart. Geometry on the left, matter on the right — now the whole equation is in view.

slides.title: Motion in Curved Spacetime
slides.voiceover: With the equation assembled, what happens to something moving through it? It follows a geodesic — the straightest possible path the geometry allows. The familiar rubber-sheet funnel is only an analogy, but the intuition is right: where the mass sits, the geometry is steep, so paths bend sharply toward it. And there is no invisible hand yanking objects inward. They simply coast, as freely and as straight as curved space permits.

slides.title: Free Fall = Straightest Possible Path
slides.voiceover: We can write that straightest path as the geodesic equation. It is really Newton's F equals m a, rewritten for curved spacetime. The role that force played in Newton's world is now played by the Christoffel symbols — those direction corrections from before. Switch off the curvature and they vanish, leaving ordinary straight lines. And the tau in the equation is simply time as measured on the falling object's own clock.

slides.title: Gravity Creates Gravity
slides.voiceover: Now we arrive at the single hardest fact in this whole subject: gravity creates more gravity. Compare it with light. In electromagnetism, a beam of light does not itself become a new source of light. Gravity is different. The energy stored in the gravitational field is itself a form of energy — so it, too, bends spacetime and generates more gravity. That self-feeding loop is exactly the non-linearity we met earlier, and it is what makes dynamic, evolving solutions almost impossible to write down by hand.

slides.title: Light Cones Near a Black Hole
slides.voiceover: Light cones show you how extreme that geometry can get. A light cone simply marks where you are allowed to go next — your possible futures. Far from a black hole, the cones stand upright and light can head outward freely. Approach the horizon and the cones tip inward. Cross it, and both edges of your cone point inward — every possible future leads deeper in. Nothing escapes a black hole, and now you can see why: not because of some suction, but because geometry has left no outward future to choose.

slides.title: Discretizing Spacetime
slides.voiceover: So here is where we stand. General relativity is elegant, it is correct, and for anything that actually moves it is impossible to solve by hand. That single sentence is the reason numerical relativity exists — so let's hand the equations to a computer. But a computer has its own limitation: it cannot hold a smooth, continuous field in its memory. So we chop space into a grid of points, and we replace every derivative with a finite difference between neighboring points. Real simulations use enormous grids, with finer grids nested near the black holes. The entire craft of numerical relativity is rewriting Einstein's equations so that this grid actually stays stable and accurate.

slides.title: Resolution Matters
slides.voiceover: And resolution is not a detail — it is everything. A coarse grid renders a smooth curve as something jagged and plainly wrong. Worse, finite differences amplify whatever error is present, which means derivatives are even harder to get right than the values themselves. That is why numerical relativity insists on fine grids and high-order stencils — fourth or even sixth order — just to keep the errors under control.

slides.title: The Equations Don't Point Forward in Time
slides.part: Part 2 — From Geometry to a Movie
slides.voiceover: There is one more obstacle, and it is a deep one. Einstein's equations treat space and time on completely equal footing. Nowhere in them is there a built-in sense of now versus next — and a computer desperately needs that. A computer wants an initial-value problem: give me the geometry now, and I will compute the geometry a moment later. So we deliberately break that four-dimensional symmetry and rewrite general relativity as three-dimensional space evolving forward through time — like the frames of a movie. Everything that follows — A D M, B S S N, C C Z four — is exactly the same physics, just with smarter bookkeeping.

slides.title: The 3+1 Decomposition
slides.voiceover: The first and most important reorganization is the A D M three-plus-one split. It takes the single spacetime metric and separates it into three pieces: a spatial metric, a lapse, and a shift — ten numbers in total, the very same information as before, just arranged for time-stepping. Think of it concretely. The spatial metric is the shape of space on one slice of now. The lapse is how fast clocks tick from one slice to the next. And the shift is how the coordinate grid slides sideways between slices.

slides.title: Slicing 4D Spacetime
slides.voiceover: Here is the picture that names the whole lecture. Imagine the entire history of the universe as a single loaf of bread. Slice it, and each slice is all of space frozen at one instant. The computer then flips from one slice to the next, like pages in a flipbook, and motion appears. That is the three-plus-one idea made visual — and it is why we call this slicing spacetime.

slides.title: Lapse, Shift & Extrinsic Curvature
slides.voiceover: Those three pieces become the quantities we actually evolve: lapse, shift, and extrinsic curvature. Each has a practical job. Near a black hole we deliberately drive the lapse toward zero, so the simulation slows down and never crashes into the singularity. The shift keeps the coordinate grid from tangling as the black holes move around. And the extrinsic curvature records how quickly the shape of each slice is stretching or squeezing as time moves on.

slides.title: Lapse & Shift in Action
slides.voiceover: In practice it looks like this. A high lapse means a lot of real, proper time passes between slices. A low lapse means clocks nearly freeze — exactly what we want near a singularity. And a nonzero shift tilts the lines that connect one slice to the next. It is worth repeating: these are coordinate tools, not new physics. We choose them purely to keep the simulation stable.

slides.title: 10 Equations → 6 + 4
slides.voiceover: Now feed that three-plus-one split back into Einstein's equations, and the original ten sort themselves into two very different families. Six of them are evolution equations — these are the ones that actually march the geometry forward in time. The other four are constraint equations, and they contain no time derivative at all. Instead, they must already be true on every single slice: one Hamiltonian constraint and three momentum constraints. The consequence is strict — if your starting data violates these constraints, it is not a valid universe, and the simulation will crash on the spot.

slides.title: ADM Evolution Equations
slides.voiceover: Look closer at those six evolution equations, because they are the beating heart of the whole scheme. The shape of space changes over time because the slice is bending. And the rate of that bending itself changes, driven by clock gradients, by the curvature already present, and by the matter. So shape drives bending, and bending in turn drives shape. That mutual feedback, resolved over and over, is exactly what every single timestep has to compute.

slides.title: Constraint Equations
slides.voiceover: The four constraint equations are best understood as the rules of Sudoku. They never tell you what your next move should be. They only tell you whether the board in front of you is legal. The Hamiltonian constraint checks that the energy budget balances. The momentum constraints check that the flow budget balances. In perfect mathematics, valid data stays valid forever. But on a real computer, tiny rounding errors leak in at every step. With the raw A D M form, those errors piled up for roughly thirty years and blew simulations apart. B S S N and C C Z four are what finally fixed the form — without ever touching the physics.

slides.title: Why ADM Crashes
slides.voiceover: So why exactly does A D M crash? A healthy numerical system needs every disturbance to travel at a definite, finite speed, so errors move along and wash out. A D M has modes that travel at zero or undefined speed — meaning numerical junk just sits in place and grows exponentially instead of leaving. Let me be clear: the physics was always correct. The packaging was simply wrong for a grid. And this is precisely why the path of progress runs A D M, then B S S N, then C C Z four.

slides.title: ADM vs BSSN: Stability
slides.voiceover: Watch the two side by side. Same initial data, same tiny speck of numerical noise seeded into both. With A D M, that noise has no speed limit, so it just sits and explodes. With B S S N, the identical noise is carried away at a finite speed and stays safely bounded. This one difference — noise that leaves versus noise that grows — is why the entire field was frozen in place for decades.

slides.title: BSSN: Same Physics, Better Variables
slides.voiceover: It is worth saying plainly what B S S N is and is not. It is not new gravity — it is a clever change of variables. We separate the overall size of space from its shape, we split the average expansion from the uneven stretching, and we evolve the coordinate-drift quantities out in the open rather than letting them hide. The payoff is exactly what we needed: every disturbance now travels at a known, finite speed, so noise is carried off the grid instead of blowing up on it.

slides.title: BSSN Evolution Equations
slides.voiceover: So instead of one hopelessly tangled set of equations, we now march a handful of clean, well-behaved pieces: the scale factor, the rescaled shape of space, the average curvature, and a few more. Numerical noise in these variables propagates harmlessly away. And this, after roughly thirty years of simulations dying, is the reason long runs finally started to survive.

slides.title: CCZ4: Constraint Damping
slides.voiceover: C C Z four adds the finishing touch — a clean-up crew. B S S N stops errors from exploding, but they can still slowly drift away from a valid solution. So C C Z four adds extra fields that continuously measure how far you have drifted, plus damping parameters that act like friction, gently dragging that error back toward zero. Here is the intuition: B S S N is a ball resting on a flat table, content to roll anywhere, while C C Z four is a ball sitting in a bowl, always pulled back to the bottom. This is what modern codes such as GRTeclyn rely on.

slides.title: Gauge Conditions
slides.voiceover: One last set of choices remains: the gauge conditions. These are free choices of coordinates, not physics — you are simply deciding how to label events. One-plus-log slicing slows clocks near a singularity, so the simulation approaches it but never actually arrives. The Gamma-driver shift steers the grid to follow the black holes as they move, like a camera panning to track a speeding car. Combine those two with B S S N and you have the moving-punctures recipe — the exact combination that finally cracked the problem open in two thousand five.

slides.title: 2005: The Binary Black Hole Problem Solved
slides.voiceover: And so we reach two thousand five, the year the binary black hole problem was finally solved. Remarkably, three groups arrived almost at once: Pretorius, using generalized harmonic coordinates, and independently Campanelli and Baker, using moving punctures together with B S S N. The recipe they converged on is still the standard today — B S S N or C C Z four, one-plus-log slicing, and a Gamma-driver shift — and it delivers a stable inspiral, merger, and ringdown from start to finish.

slides.title: Binary Black Hole Merger
slides.voiceover: This is what all that machinery finally lets us compute. First the inspiral: the orbit tightens and the frequency climbs — the famous chirp — as energy pours out as gravitational waves. Then the merger: the loudest, fully non-linear peak, the exact moment that defeated every hand calculation. And finally the ringdown: the newborn remnant rings like a struck bell as it settles. That complete waveform is what LIGO compares against — and without numerical relativity, the merger in the middle is simply missing.

slides.title: LIGO — Laser Interferometer
slides.voiceover: On the other end sits LIGO, the detector that receives these waveforms. It is a Michelson interferometer with two arms, each four kilometers long, set at right angles. A laser is split, sent down both arms, and recombined — and it is tuned so that normally the output is dark. When a gravitational wave passes, it stretches one arm and squeezes the other by about one part in ten to the twenty-one — an almost absurdly tiny amount — and that shifts the interference pattern just enough to see. The first detection was two black holes merging about one point three billion light-years away, and it earned the Nobel Prize in two thousand seventeen — made possible by numerical templates.

slides.title: Gravitational Wave Polarization
slides.voiceover: Those waves also come in two flavors, called polarizations: plus and cross. The plus polarization stretches and squeezes space along the two axes. The cross polarization does exactly the same thing, but rotated by forty-five degrees. Place a ring of free-floating particles in the path of the wave and you would watch it flex in these patterns as the wave rolls through. LIGO's two perpendicular arms are shaped precisely to catch the plus polarization.

slides.title: Convergence Testing
slides.voiceover: Which raises a fair and uncomfortable question: how do we know the simulation is real physics and not elaborate numerical garbage? A grid is only ever an approximation, so we test it honestly. We run the exact same problem three times, at three different resolutions. If the code is genuinely correct, the differences between those solutions must shrink at a specific, predictable rate — the rate set by the order of our finite differences.

slides.title: Richardson Extrapolation
slides.voiceover: That predictable rate is captured in a single sanity-check number, the convergence factor Q. For a fourth-order scheme, Q should march toward sixteen. If instead Q comes out wild and erratic, it is a red flag: either there is a bug in the code, or the run simply does not have enough resolution. GRTeclyn uses fourth-order stencils, so the number we are watching for is Q approaching sixteen.

slides.title: Convergence in Action
slides.voiceover: And here it is in action. The coarse, medium, and fine solutions all draw together as the resolution improves, and the Q readout climbs toward sixteen, right on target for a fourth-order code. This is the moment we get to trust the result — the evidence that what we are seeing is real physics and not a numerical artifact.

slides.title: CPU Islands vs GPU Megacity
slides.voiceover: Trustworthy answers still take enormous power to produce, which brings us to hardware. Picture C P U cores as separate islands that have to talk over slow bridges — information stalls in transit. A G P U instead packs thousands of cores that all share one fast pool of memory, more like a dense megacity. This matters because every numerical relativity update is a stencil: each grid cell reads its neighbors at every step. When neighbors live in the same fast shared memory, the whole calculation transforms.

slides.title: Why the GPU Wins
slides.voiceover: The numbers make that advantage concrete. A data-center G P U, like an H one hundred, carries far more parallel cores and roughly seven times the memory bandwidth of a large server C P U. And for stencil codes like ours, that bandwidth — how fast you can feed the cores their neighbors — matters far more than raw floating-point peak. The bottom line is striking: a single G P U can replace an entire rack of C P Us.

slides.title: Riding the AI Wave
slides.voiceover: And this is where our opening timeline finally closes its loop. The very same hardware that the world is racing to build in order to train large A I models is, atom for atom, exactly what solves Einstein's equations. Numerical relativity gets to ride the entire A I hardware boom for free — cheaper operations, bigger memory, faster interconnects — without ever having to fund a single chip of its own.

slides.title: AMR: Resolution Where It Matters
slides.voiceover: One more trick stretches that power even further: adaptive mesh refinement. Rather than paying for a fine grid everywhere, we place fine grids only where curvature is fierce — right around the black holes and along the wave fronts — and leave coarse grids in the calm regions far away. That single idea cuts the cost by orders of magnitude. Without adaptive mesh refinement, a realistic binary or wormhole run would simply be out of reach.

slides.title: GRTeclyn / AMReX
slides.voiceover: The code that ties all of this together is GRTeclyn, from the GRChombo family. It is built for GPUs from the ground up, and it stands on A M ReX to handle the adaptive mesh refinement, the load balancing, and the offloading of work onto the GPU. This is the practical tool that makes simulating exotic spacetimes feasible at all — and it takes us straight into the research.

slides.title: Traversable Wormholes
slides.voiceover: Here is the research payoff. A traversable wormhole is a tunnel through spacetime, held open by exotic matter — hypothetical stuff with negative energy that pushes space apart rather than pulling it together. Normal matter would attract and pinch the tunnel shut; the exotic support works like anti-gravity, bracing the throat open. And that sets up the question we actually simulate: what happens the moment that support is taken away?

slides.title: Wormhole Collapse
slides.voiceover: So in the simulation, we strip away most of the exotic support and give the throat a slight squeeze. Gravity immediately wins: the throat collapses, and an apparent horizon forms. But the story is not over — the exotic matter that gets swallowed fights back in what we call a phantom bounce, a violent rebound of curvature. And because the collapse is not perfectly symmetric, it flings gravitational waves outward at nearly the speed of light.

slides.title: What We Found
slides.voiceover: So what did we actually find? The headline is this: a single exotic object can radiate a gravitational-wave signal all on its own, with no collision at all. For the squeeze strength we studied, a roughly one-thousand-solar-mass wormhole at about one megaparsec sits right near the sensitivity of Advanced LIGO. To detect it reliably we would want closer sources, larger asymmetries, or the next generation of detectors. The full details are in the paper on the archive, number two six zero four point zero zero zero seven one.

slides.title: Rotating Wormhole & Q-Ball Torus
slides.voiceover: There is an even cleaner version: the rotating case. Here a spinning ring of exotic matter is what holds the throat open. Turn off the pump, and something nice happens automatically — the rotation has already broken the spherical symmetry, so the collapse naturally emits a strong, coherent burst of gravitational waves, with no artificial squeeze required at all.

slides.title: From Pen & Paper to Digital Laboratories
slides.voiceover: Let me leave you with the whole journey in four steps. First, general relativity broke unaided human calculation — ten non-linear equations that feed on themselves. Second, A D M slicing turned that four-dimensional physics into a three-dimensional movie a computer could play. Third, two thousand five, with B S S N, C C Z four, and the right gauge choices, finally made that movie stable. And fourth, GPUs and adaptive mesh refinement supplied the raw power to explore even exotic spacetimes. The result: in two thousand fifteen we heard two black holes merge. And tomorrow, we just might catch a wormhole collapsing.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.voiceover: Thank you. I am happy to take any questions.
